ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for char siew roasted pork noodle in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of char siew and roasted pork noodle in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles topped with sliced char siew, siu yuk, and dressed with dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew & Roasted Pork Noodle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Char Siew & Roasted Pork Noodle (叉烧烧肉面). The HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which reflects the meat-and-sauce dominant analytical portion; the full dish with noodles carries significant carbohydrate load not captured in this entry. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew and roasted pork noodle as a composite dish. The HPB-reported carbohydrate for this entry is 0.0 g/100g (meat-and-sauce analytical portion), making a meaningful GI calculation for the meat component not applicable. However, the full dish as consumed includes egg noodles, which have a GI of approximately 52 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles / Chinese-style wheat noodles. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; because the HPB carbohydrate figure for this entry is 0.0 g/100g, the GL is reported as 0.0 for the analytical portion. Consumers should note that the actual dish with noodles carries a meaningful carbohydrate load and glycemic impact. GI of 52 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on egg noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 1050 mg/100g, driven by dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, hoisin sauce, and char siew glaze; potassium at 47 mg/100g provides negligible offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request minimal sauce, choose a smaller portion, and avoid both char siew and siu yuk on the same serving, as both are sodium-dense cured/roasted pork products.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g for the meat-dominant analytical portion, but the full dish with egg noodles carries a significant refined-carbohydrate load; char siew glaze (honey/maltose) adds additional sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion or substitute with whole-grain noodles, ask for less char siew glaze to reduce sugar intake, and account for the full carbohydrate load of the noodle component when calculating insulin or medication dosing.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (10.5 g/100g) from pork and lard, high cholesterol (89 mg/100g), extremely high sodium (1050 mg/100g), and processed/cured pork components with inherent saturated fat and AGE load from high-temperature roasting.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this dish due to its combination of high sodium, high cholesterol, and saturated fat from cured and roasted pork; if consumed, choose a smaller portion, request less sauce, and remove pork skin to reduce saturated fat and AGE exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Both pork components are red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature roasting and caramelisation generate significant advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with chicken or fish, remove pork skin to reduce saturated fat and AGE load, minimise char siew due to its high caramelisation-derived AGE content, and request less sauce to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew and Roasted Pork Noodle (叉烧烧肉面) is a popular Cantonese-origin noodle dish widely eaten in Singapore as a hawker-centre staple for lunch and dinner. The dish consists of springy egg noodles — typically thin, alkaline-treated wheat noodles — served dry or in broth and topped with two distinct pork preparations: char siew (叉烧), which is Chinese-style barbecued pork marinated in a sweet-savoury glaze of honey, maltose, hoisin sauce, and five-spice powder then roasted until caramelised; and siu yuk (烧肉), which is Cantonese crispy roasted pork belly with crackling skin. The noodles are dressed with dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, lard or sesame oil, and sometimes a light stock. Bean sprouts and spring onions are common garnishes. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly sweet from the char siew glaze, with textural contrast between the tender BBQ pork, the crispy roasted pork skin, and the chewy noodles. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records this dish at 171 kcal/100g with high protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate fat (10.5 g/100g); the carbohydrate figure of 0.0 g/100g reflects the meat-and-sauce analytical portion only and does not represent the full dish as consumed with noodles. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The dish is not halal due to its pork content. Clinically, the combination of high sodium, processed cured pork, saturated fat, and high-temperature roasting-derived AGEs makes this dish high-concern for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis, and moderate-concern for type 2 diabetes when the noodle carbohydrate load is accounted for.

GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on egg noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving is reported as 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g for the analytical portion; actual GL for the full dish with noodles will be substantially higher.
